text: Male recreational marathon runners are more likely to hit the wall (significant late-race slowing) than female runners, with 28% of males versus 17% of females experiencing sustained pace collapse.

Hitting the wall is more frequent in the 3 years immediately before and after a runner achieves a personal best, with 36% of runners hitting the wall in the 3 years before a PB compared to 23% in earlier years.
When hitting the wall, male runners experience a greater relative pace slowdown than female runners (0.40 vs 0.37 ratio comparing wall pace to earlier 5km-20km pace), though the effect size is small.
Male runners who hit the wall slow over longer distances than female runners (10.7 km vs 9.6 km of sustained slowing), though the effect size is small.
The finish-time cost of hitting the wall (minutes lost) increases with runner ability, meaning faster runners lose more time in absolute terms when they experience late-race pace collapse.
Runners pushing toward a personal best face a higher risk-reward tradeoff: aggressive pacing near PB fitness increases wall-hitting probability, suggesting conservative early pacing is protective.
Hitting the wall typically occurs after the 20-mile (32 km) mark and involves sustained pace deterioration driven primarily by glycogen depletion.
